title: Two Lineages in One Code
text:
Open the PPC anywhere and you are reading two different legal traditions stitched into one document. The outer architecture — General Part (S.1–75: definitions, general exceptions, abetment, attempt) followed by a Special Part enumerating named offences (S.299 onward) — is pure 19th-century Anglo-common-law codification. Lord Macaulay drafted the original 1860 Code drawing on English criminal law and the continental codification movements of his era, organizing crime the way English jurists organized it: define terms once (S.6–S.52, General Explanations), state universal defences once (S.76–S.106, General Exceptions), then list specific offences without repeating the general apparatus each time. This is why S.6 exists at all — it has to tell you, explicitly, that every offence definition is automatically read subject to the Exceptions chapter, because nothing about the offence definitions themselves would tell you that.

Then, starting in 1979 (the Hudood Ordinances) and accelerating through 1990 (the Qisas and Diyat Ordinance, prompted by the Federal Shariat Court’s 1980 ruling in Muhammad Riaz v. Federation that the original S.302 was repugnant to Islam), a second, older legal tradition was grafted onto that colonial skeleton: classical Islamic criminal jurisprudence. S.299–S.338H — the entire Qatl and Hurt apparatus, with its Qisas, Diyat, Arsh, Daman, and Ta’zir vocabulary — did not exist in Macaulay’s 1860 draft in this form at all. It was inserted wholesale, decades later, into a structure built for a different jurisprudential logic.

The seam between these two traditions is visible if you know where to look. S.53’s punishment list (Punishments module) reads almost schizophrenically once you notice it: Qisas, Diyat, Arsh, Daman sit beside Death, Imprisonment, Forfeiture, Fine — two vocabularies from two different legal universes, listed as if they were interchangeable options on a single menu. They are not. The colonial vocabulary (Ta’zir, Death, Imprisonment, Fine) assumes the state is the only relevant party. The classical vocabulary (Qisas, Diyat, Arsh, Daman) assumes the victim or their heirs are the primary party, with the state in a supporting role. S.305’s 2005 proviso (Qatl Core module, excluding the offender from wali status in honour cases) only makes sense once you see this seam — it is a 2005 patch repairing a 1990 graft that had been sitting, unexamined, on top of a 1860 frame.

This matters practically, not just historically. When you read any section of this Code, the first question worth asking is which lineage it belongs to. General Exceptions, General Explanations, Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy, the entire structural architecture of how offences are defined (S.39 ‘voluntarily’, S.40 ‘offence’, the actus reus/mens rea apparatus) — common law. Qatl, Hurt, their entire Qisas/Diyat/Arsh/Daman compensation and punishment machinery — classical fiqh, reactivated. Marriage offences, Hudood-adjacent provisions — also largely classical-law-derived, inserted in the same 1979–1990 wave. Knowing which lineage governs a given section tells you what kind of reasoning will actually work on it: common-law sections respond to textual, definitional, precedent-based argument; classical-law sections respond to a different style of reasoning entirely, one this app’s next essay tries to make visible.
reflect: Pick any offence module you’ve already studied in this app. Can you tell, just from how it’s drafted, whether it descends from the 1860 common-law frame or the 1979/1990 classical-law graft? What gave it away?

If you were advising a foreign lawyer unfamiliar with Pakistani law, how would you explain in two sentences why the PPC sometimes reasons like an English statute and sometimes reasons like a different legal tradition entirely?

title: Huquq Allah and Huquq al-‘Ibad: Two Rights, Two Logics
text:
Every offence in the PPC quietly assumes an answer to one question: whose claim is this? Classical Islamic jurisprudence sorts wrongs into two categories that map onto almost nothing in the common-law tradition the Code’s outer architecture borrows from. Huquq Allah (Rights of God) are public-order claims — offences the community as a whole has a stake in, non-waivable by any individual, prosecuted regardless of any private party’s wishes. Huquq al-‘Ibad (Rights of Humans) are private claims belonging to a specific rights-holder, who can waive, forgive, or settle them.

Qatl sits asymmetrically across this divide, and this asymmetry is the single most useful lens for understanding why Chapter XVI (S.299–S.338H) behaves so differently from every other chapter in this app. The remedy of Qisas is a Haqq al-‘Ibad — it belongs to the wali (S.305, defining who holds it), which is exactly why S.309 lets them waive it unconditionally and S.310 lets them compound it for Badal-i-Sulh. But classical fiqh, particularly in Hanafi and Maliki reasoning, has long recognized a residual societal interest — sometimes termed haqq al-jama’a — in not letting deliberate killing go entirely without consequence, even where the private right has been settled. This residual interest is not a modern secular invention; it is why Ta’zir discretion for the ruler or qadi to additionally punish, even after Diyat is paid, has classical roots reaching back centuries before Pakistan existed as a state.

This reframes S.311 (Murder module) entirely. It is not the modern state importing a foreign ‘crimes against the state’ concept onto a purely private classical framework. It is the legislature reactivating a Ta’zir-based public-interest backstop that classical fiqh already contained in latent form — specifically because the wali-forgiveness mechanism (S.309/S.310) was being captured by the very people benefiting from the killing, most visibly in honour killings where the offender’s own relatives held the wali’s forgiveness power. S.311’s fasad-fil-arz override does not abolish the private right; it layers the dormant public-interest dimension back on top of it.

Once you have this framework, Schedule II’s compoundability flags — the bare true/false badges sitting on every offence card throughout this app — stop being arbitrary data and start being a map. A compoundable offence (Schedule II marks it so) is one the legislature treats as substantially a Haqq al-‘Ibad — private enough that the victim’s forgiveness can end the matter. A non-compoundable offence is one the legislature treats as carrying enough Haqq Allah weight that private forgiveness cannot extinguish state interest. Go back through the offence modules already built — Theft, Hurt’s various tiers, Defamation’s S.500–502 — and the compoundable/non-compoundable pattern is not random. It is the legislature’s century-and-a-half-old answer, dispersed across hundreds of individual sections, to the same question this essay opened with: whose claim is this?
reflect: Go back through Schedule II’s compoundability flags on any module you’ve built. Pick one offence marked compoundable that surprised you, and one marked non-compoundable that surprised you. What does each tell you about whose right the legislature considered it to be?

Is there a version of the Huquq Allah / Huquq al-‘Ibad distinction in how you already intuitively separate civil from criminal wrongs — or does this framework cut the line somewhere you wouldn’t have expected?

title: S.311 — A Worked Example: Daman, Diyat, and the Wealthy Offender
meta: Essay 3 of 18 · Cross-linked from the Murder module
text:
Take everything in the first two essays and run it through one concrete provision: S.311 PPC, the fasad-fil-arz mechanism (Murder module). The drafting itself encodes the entire jurisprudential argument, if you read it closely enough.

Start with Diyat’s structure (S.323, Qatl Core module). Diyat has a hard floor — the value of 30,630 grams of silver, revalued annually by Federal Government gazette notification — but no ceiling stated in S.323 itself. Why a floor and not a ceiling? Because Diyat is meant to be a serious, meaningful compensation to the wali for a genuine loss, not a fee schedule. The floor prevents a token, insulting payment; the absence of a stated ceiling leaves room for courts (and parties negotiating Badal-i-Sulh under S.310) to scale upward for aggravating circumstances. Compare this to Daman (S.299(d), the general compensation category for hurt not liable to Arsh, and S.337Y’s valuation criteria for Hurt) — Daman has neither a fixed floor nor a fixed ceiling at all. It is pure judicial discretion, calibrated case-by-case to actual treatment costs, functional loss, and the victim’s anguish. The contrast is instructive: Diyat is a quantified private right with a known value because it is meant to function as a real, substantial, but ultimately fixed-and-knowable settlement between private parties. Daman’s open-endedness reflects a court actively calibrating to individual circumstance rather than executing a known private bargain.

Now place a wealthy or tribally powerful offender into this structure. Pre-S.311, the danger was structural, not hypothetical: a wealthy offender’s family could pay Diyat — even Diyat scaled generously upward — essentially as a transaction cost of killing, particularly where the killing was honour-motivated and the wali themselves were the offender’s own relatives, predisposed to forgive. Diyat’s waivability, designed to enable genuine reconciliation between grieving families, was being repurposed as a price list for the powerful. This is precisely the fasad-fil-arz (‘corruption on earth’) concept S.311 invokes — not a vague, free-floating moral category, but a specific classical-jurisprudence response to exactly this abuse: wealth or power converting a private right of forgiveness into impunity for the gravest crime in the Code.

S.311’s text answers this with a deliberately unconstrained instrument: where fasad-fil-arz is attracted, the court ‘may’ impose Ta’zir of death, life imprisonment, or up to fourteen years — but where the honour-killing proviso specifically applies, that discretion collapses to a single mandatory outcome, life imprisonment, regardless of what Diyat was paid or how completely the wali forgave. There is no silver-weight floor here, no negotiable settlement — because this is precisely the point in the analysis where the law deliberately exits the private, Huquq al-‘Ibad register Diyat operates in, and re-enters the public, Huquq Allah-adjacent register where no quantity of private wealth can purchase exit. S.305’s 2005 amendment (excluding the offender from wali status in honour cases) and S.310’s 2005 proviso barring a female as Badal-i-Sulh are the same instinct applied at earlier points in the same sequence — each one closing a specific door through which wealth, power, or family control could turn private forgiveness into a purchasable escape.
reflect: If you were drafting S.311 from scratch, knowing what you now know about Diyat’s floor-but-no-ceiling structure, would you have built the honour-killing override as a mandatory single outcome (life imprisonment) or kept it as bounded discretion? What does your answer reveal about how much you trust judicial discretion versus legislative certainty in cases like this?

Daman has no fixed floor or ceiling at all — pure discretion. Diyat has a floor but no ceiling. Qisas, where executable, has neither — it’s a fixed equivalence. Why might the law trust judges with more discretion in some of these and less in others?

title: From Aristocratic Mecca to Ta’zir: An Arc Across Fourteen Centuries
meta: Essay 4 of 18 · Extends the Theories of Punishment Evolutionary Arc
text:
The Theories of Punishment module already traces three eras within Pakistan’s own 165-year legal history — colonial deterrence (1860–1990), Islamic retributive-restorative reintroduction (1990 onward), and state re-intervention against restorative-mechanism abuse (2005–present, the S.305/S.311/S.336B/S.337M(2) sequence). This essay reaches further back, to the social context the second of those eras was actually restoring.

Pre-Islamic Mecca operated on tribal, aristocratic custom: blood-price (diya) was a matter of raw negotiating power between clans, not a fixed or principled figure. A powerful, wealthy tribe could often escape meaningful consequence for killing a member of a weaker one, simply by virtue of negotiating leverage — the blood-feud alternative being so much costlier that lopsided settlements were routinely accepted under duress. Early Islamic legal reform did not invent the concept of compensable killing; it inherited diya from this Arabian tribal context and did something specific to it: it constrained the arbitrariness. Fixing Diyat’s value (the silver-weight standard S.323 still uses today), structuring the wali’s choice as a defined legal right rather than an open negotiation, and layering the qadi’s residual Ta’zir authority on top — all of this took a custom that had been a pure function of tribal power and gave it a structured, rights-based, and partially state-supervised form.

The 1979–1990 Pakistani reintroduction (Hudood Ordinances, then the Qisas and Diyat Ordinance) was explicitly reaching back to this classical structure, not inventing a new one. But the 2005–2016 amendment sequence — S.305, S.310’s anti-vani proviso, S.311, S.324’s honour-killing floor, S.336B’s acid-attack mandatory minimum, S.337M(2)’s recidivism/honour floor, S.338E’s chapter-wide extension — is best understood as a second-order correction: Pakistan discovered, empirically, in the 1990s and 2000s, that the very abuse classical Islamic law had originally constrained in 7th-century Arabia (wealthy or powerful family networks converting a victim’s death into a negotiated, low-cost settlement) was re-emerging under the modern statutory framework, particularly in honour-killing cases where family control over the wali role recreated exactly the aristocratic-leverage dynamic the classical reforms had targeted in the first place.

Seen this way, 2005–2016 is not a rupture from classical jurisprudence, nor a purely modern secular intervention bolted onto it. It is the same corrective instinct — preventing power and wealth from converting a serious private right into an impunity mechanism — being exercised a second time, under modern conditions, using the modern state’s own institutional tools (Federal legislation, the Council of Islamic Ideology’s consultative role under S.338G, Gazette-notified silver valuation) rather than 7th-century Arabian customary correction. The arc is long, but it bends in a single, consistent direction: toward closing the gap between formal legal right and actual power that could otherwise hollow that right out.
reflect: The essay frames 2005–2016 as a ‘second-order correction’ echoing a 7th-century one. Do you find that framing persuasive, or does it risk over-romanticizing a modern legislative response by giving it ancient pedigree it may not actually need to justify itself?

If a future amendment wave responds to some new form of the same underlying problem — power or wealth converting a legal right into practical impunity — where in today’s PPC would you expect the next crack to show first?

title: Why a ‘General Part’ Exists, and Why Agreeing to Commit a Crime Is Itself a Crime
text:
Two structural choices in this Code are easy to take for granted and worth pausing on, because both reveal a specific, debatable theory of how law should reason and what law should prevent.

First: why does General Exceptions (S.76–S.106) exist as its own separate chapter, rather than each defence being written directly into its relevant offence? This is a distinctly common-law, codification-era choice, not a universal feature of criminal law systems. Classical fiqh, by contrast, does not generally organize defences this way — reasoning tends to proceed offence-by-offence, with relevant exceptions and conditions discussed within the treatment of that specific offence, rather than abstracted into a standalone, universally-applicable general doctrine. Macaulay’s separation reflects an Enlightenment-codification assumption: that legal reasoning works best when general principles are stated once, abstractly, and then mechanically applied across every specific case — the same logical instinct behind General Explanations’ definitional apparatus (S.6–S.52) and behind S.6 itself, which has to explicitly instruct readers that Exceptions silently attach to every offence definition, precisely because the architecture separates the two and risks a reader missing the connection. This is also why this app’s own General Exceptions module needed its own deep-dive expansion — the abstraction that makes the doctrine elegant on paper (one unified chapter of defences) also makes it easy to under-examine, since no single offence module forces you to confront S.94’s duress exception or S.84’s unsound-mind standard the way you are forced to confront, say, S.300’s mens rea clauses when studying Murder directly.

Second: why does the Code punish Abetment (S.107–S.120) and Criminal Conspiracy (S.120-A/120-B) as offences in their own right, when nothing has actually happened yet — no death, no theft, no completed harm of any kind? This is pure common-law inchoate-liability theory, with essentially no equivalent driving force in classical fiqh’s traditional treatment of crime, which tends to focus on completed, identifiable wrongs against specific persons. The common-law justification, traceable through 19th and 20th century criminal-law theory (the kind of reasoning later systematized by writers like Glanville Williams), is harm-prevention through early intervention: society has a legitimate interest in stopping dangerous conduct before it produces its intended harm, not merely in punishing harm after the fact. Abetment criminalizes instigating, conspiring toward, or aiding an offence — punishing the abettor’s own culpable conduct, independent of whether the principal offence actually succeeds. Criminal Conspiracy goes further still, criminalizing the bare agreement itself, complete at the moment of agreement, before any further act toward the goal has been taken at all.

Notice that this inchoate-liability theory sits entirely within the colonial, common-law lineage this whole essay series has been tracing — it is not a graft from classical jurisprudence the way Qatl and Hurt’s machinery is. Abetment and Conspiracy are, in this specific sense, ‘purer’ common law than almost anything else in the Code: no Qisas, no Diyat, no wali, no fasad-fil-arz — just the state, acting alone, against conduct that has not yet caused anyone any harm at all, justified entirely by a theory of prevention rather than a theory of compensation or retribution. If the first four essays in this series taught you to ask which lineage a section belongs to, Abetment and Conspiracy are the cleanest possible test case: read them, and ask yourself honestly whether their underlying logic feels like Qatl’s logic, or like something else entirely. It is something else entirely — and seeing that clearly is the real payoff of this whole rabbit hole.
reflect: Try rewriting one offence you’ve studied (any module) as if General Exceptions didn’t exist as a separate chapter — fold the relevant defence directly into the offence definition instead. Does the offence become clearer or more confusing to read that way?

Abetment and Conspiracy punish conduct before any harm occurs, justified by prevention rather than retribution or compensation. Does that justification feel as solid to you as the reasoning behind Qatl’s framework — or does inchoate liability rest on shakier philosophical ground?

title: The Unification Machine — One Law for Many Gods
text:
There is a single sentence in Macaulay’s 1837 Prefatory Address to the Governor-General that contains the entire intellectual architecture of everything that followed. It is not the most famous sentence he ever wrote. But it may be the most consequential.

“It appears to us that none of the systems of penal law established in British India has any claim to our attention, except what it may derive from its own intrinsic excellence.”

Read that again slowly. He is not saying Islamic law is wrong. He is not saying Hindu law is inferior. He is saying that all three systems — Hindu dharmashastra, Islamic sharia, and the existing British Regulations — are equally disqualified as foundations for the new Code. Not because they are foreign impositions on an authentic Indian people, but because, as he immediately observes, they are all foreign impositions: “All those systems are foreign. All were introduced by conquerors differing in race, manners, language and religion from the great mass of the people.”

With a single paragraph, Macaulay levelled the playing field. Hindu law had been superseded by Islamic law, which had in turn been distorted and partially superseded by British Regulations across three separate Presidencies that contradicted each other on almost every major question. In Bengal, forgeries received double the imprisonment of perjury. In Bombay, perjury received double the imprisonment of forgeries. In Madras they were the same. This was the legal order he inherited — not a system but three competing bureaucratic accidents, each calling itself law.

His solution was not a compromise. It was an erasure. He took nothing from any of the three systems as its “groundwork,” as he explicitly states. What he borrowed from each, he chose for its intrinsic merit on the specific question at hand — drawing on the French Code, on Edward Livingston’s Louisiana Code, on Benthamite legal philosophy — and assembled something new. Something that could govern a Hindu and a Muslim and a Sikh and a British soldier with the same words.

But here is the insight that most commentators miss, and that directly answers the question of how he achieved it: the reason a single code could govern multiple religious communities was not that Macaulay negotiated between their moral systems. It was that he refused to engage their moral systems at all. The morality was silent. Deliberate, principled, architectural silence — a subject this series returns to in its own dedicated essay.

Look at how the provision that became S.295 of the 1860 Code is written: “Whoever destroys, damages or defiles any place of worship, or any object held sacred by any class of persons, with the intention of thereby insulting the religion of any class of persons...” The law does not say which religion is right. It treats religious feeling as a social fact — a fact that, when provoked, produces violence and disorder — and it protects religious feeling precisely because provoking it breaks the peace. Not because any faith is true. A mosque is protected from desecration under S.295 for exactly the same legal reason a temple is. The harm is the social consequence. The morality is left unstated.

This is the move that made the Code politically possible. Macaulay could not produce a code that said “thou shalt not commit adultery because God forbids it” in a society where different communities had different Gods with different opinions about adultery. But he could produce a code that said “thou shalt not commit adultery because the law forbids it and a penalty attaches” — and leave each community to supply its own moral justification from within its own tradition. The Hindu could add: “and God forbids it too.” The Muslim could add: “and Allah forbids it too.” The secular Englishman could add: “and rational utilitarianism counsels against it.” None of them needed to agree with each other. They all needed to obey the same law.

This is Bentham’s genius filtered through Macaulay’s pen: define the act, specify the consequence, and leave the moral justification to the individual conscience. The law does not ask why you think murder is wrong. It simply tells you what happens to you if you commit it.

The practical consequence of this design choice reverberated through every subsequent political transition on this subcontinent. In 1947, when Pakistan emerged with an explicitly Islamic identity, the Code survived because its architecture was neutral — it had no Christian moral baggage, nothing that required a philosophical fight to keep. It simply described acts and consequences. It could be adopted, wholesale, by an Islamic republic without anyone needing to pretend that Macaulay had been a crypto-Muslim.

Then, in 1979, Zia-ul-Haq broke the silence. The Hudood Ordinances and subsequently the Qisas and Diyat Ordinance of 1990 introduced something the Code had never contained: punishment calibrated to the religious identity of the offender and the victim. For the first time since 1860, the law asked: what is your religion? And your answer changed the consequences.

This was not merely a change of content. It was a change of architecture. Macaulay had built a roof that covered everyone precisely by not marking anyone’s territory beneath it. The 1979 intervention punched a hole in the roof and said: this part belongs to Islamic law alone. The hole has been patched, partially, by subsequent amendments — the 2006 Women’s Protection Act, the application of S.311 in the Qandeel Baloch case, the 2021 reforms. But the hole was made. The silence was broken.

Whether breaking that silence was more honest about Pakistani social reality than Macaulay’s deliberate neutrality is a genuine question, not a settled one. Perhaps a law governing a society that is 97% Muslim was always more honest when it said so explicitly. Perhaps the pretence of neutrality was itself a kind of colonial fiction. These remain open. What is not open is the mechanism by which the Code worked for as long as it did: one law for many gods, because the law refused to ask which god was right.
reflect: The Code’s morality is silent on religious identity. Pakistani criminal law’s largest subsequent changes — the Hudood Ordinances, Qisas and Diyat — broke that silence. Was breaking it more honest about the society being governed, or did it lose something valuable? Which political arrangement does a good criminal code ultimately serve — the state that enacts it, or the society that lives under it?

Macaulay explicitly condemned all three existing legal systems as unsuitable foundations for the new Code. He was describing legal and administrative chaos, not the content of the religions themselves. Is there a difference — legally and philosophically — between dismissing a legal system and dismissing the religion it came from?

title: The Riot at the River — What the British Were Really Protecting
text:
In 1837, when Macaulay’s Law Commission was finalising its draft, there was no running water in any Indian city. No municipal pipes, no public taps, no chlorinated reservoirs. People drank from wells, rivers, ponds, and the communal tanks that mosques maintained for ablution outside their doors. In a society where your only access to water was a shared physical place, water sources were among the most socially charged locations in the country — and among the most dangerous.

The mosque’s ablution pond was where Muslims performed wudu before prayer. The river ghat was where Hindus bathed, washed the dead, and performed the rituals of daily life. The village well was where women gathered twice a day, where community structure expressed itself physically in full view of everyone who mattered. These were not merely functional infrastructure. They were the architecture of communal identity — and in a society where Hindu and Muslim communities lived in close proximity, they were the most plausible flashpoints for exactly the kind of riots the colonial administration feared above everything else.

Now read Chapter XIV of the 1837 draft — “Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety and Convenience.” Clause 264: “Whoever, by any act, or by omitting to take order with property in his possession, voluntarily causes the atmosphere in any public way to be in a state noxious to health or offensive to the senses, shall be punished...” This became S.277 and S.278 of the enacted 1860 Code and survives, substantially unchanged, in the Pakistan Penal Code today.

The standard reading of these provisions is that they represent an early form of environmental protection — the British, supposedly, concerned about the condition of natural water sources and atmospheric pollution.

This reading is wrong. Or rather, it is incomplete in a way that misses the entire point.

The British in 1837 were not environmentalists. They were cutting down millions of trees to lay railway tracks across the subcontinent, extracting timber, coal, cotton, and indigo at industrial scale, and showing no systematic concern for the natural environment as such. They were, however, extraordinarily concerned about communal violence. The administrative record of British India in the first half of the 19th century is dense with reports about Hindu-Muslim riots, and the most common triggers for those riots were: religious processions that passed through the other community’s territory, sacred animals slaughtered or desecrated near the other community’s places of worship, and the fouling of water sources that the other community held sacred or used for religious purposes.

A Hindu who poured cow’s blood into the ablution pond of a mosque was not committing an environmental violation. He was throwing a match into a tinderbox. A Muslim who defiled a Hindu bathing ghat near a temple was not polluting a waterway. He was committing a provocation that could cascade, within hours, into mob violence that the thin administrative machinery of British India would struggle to contain.

This is what Macaulay was regulating. Not the river. The relationship between communities around the river.

The same insight applies to the Chapter XV provisions on religion — the place of worship protections, the disturbance of religious assembly provisions, the wounding of religious feelings section, and most remarkably Clause 285: “Whoever intentionally causes any food belonging to any person to be in a state in which that person, according to the rules of his religion or caste, cannot use it as food, shall be punished.” This is the provision that makes it a criminal offence to secretly mix beef into a Hindu’s food, or pork into a Muslim’s food, or to otherwise manipulate the food of a religiously observant person against their will. The law does not say these dietary rules are correct. It treats them as facts about the person — facts that, when violated, cause harm and provoke retaliation. It protects observance without validating the observance.

There is also a provision — Clause 283 in the 1837 draft, still visible in the current S.295 family — addressing Dhurna: the ancient Hindu custom of sitting at someone’s door with the intention of causing it to be believed that, by so sitting, you render that person an object of divine displeasure. Dhurna was a form of coercive social pressure operating through the threat of supernatural consequence. Macaulay criminalised it. Not because he was anti-Hindu. But because, in a multi-religious society where different communities had incompatible theologies, using the threat of divine displeasure to coerce your neighbour’s behaviour was a particularly destabilising instrument. The provision doesn’t say divine displeasure isn’t real. It says you cannot use the threat of it to compel. The silent morality principle, operating at its finest.

The pattern across the entire 1837 chapter structure is consistent: Macaulay’s real object was social order in a religiously pluralist society living around shared infrastructure. Every provision nominally about sanitation, public health, religion, or caste was designed because that content corresponded to a real friction point between communities. The sanitation language was the vehicle. Communal peace was the cargo.

What makes this historically significant, and directly relevant to the law you are now studying, is that the social conditions Macaulay was legislating for have not entirely disappeared. Pakistan in the 21st century still has water disputes. It still has tensions between communities around shared public resources. The specific communities have shifted and the specific compositions have changed, but the underlying social dynamic — shared infrastructure, contested sacred space, the ease with which a physical provocation becomes a communal confrontation — has not changed as dramatically as the political map suggests.

S.277 and S.278 survived 185 years because the problem they addressed survived 185 years. They were not protecting the environment. They were protecting the peace around a pond.
reflect: If you were advising a government today on designing public health law for a society with similar communal dynamics to 1837 British India, would you replicate Macaulay’s approach — framing communal-peace provisions as sanitation law — or would you legislate the communal-peace objective directly? What are the advantages and risks of each approach?

Macaulay criminalised Dhurna — threatening someone with divine displeasure to coerce behaviour — without taking any position on whether divine displeasure is theologically real. Can you identify a current provision in the PPC that performs the same operation: protecting a social fact without validating or denying the underlying belief system that produces it?

title: The Man Who Wasn’t a Lawyer — Why Macaulay’s Outsider Status Made the Code Great
text:
There is a line in Macaulay’s Prefatory Address that legal scholars have quoted with approval for 185 years without always registering how astonishing it is: “We have not adopted a single provision merely because it formed a part of any of those systems.”

Not one provision. From any existing system. The entire code was built from first principles, using the French Code and the Louisiana Code and Benthamite philosophy as tools for thinking, not as sources for copying.

He was able to do this because he was not a practising lawyer. He had been called to the bar at Lincoln’s Inn in 1826 but never practiced. He was a historian, a politician, and an extraordinarily well-read man who had spent years thinking about how societies are organised and why they fail. He arrived in India in 1834 as the Law Member of the Governor-General’s Council with no stake in any existing legal tradition, no guild loyalty to common law, no career investment in any existing system, and no obligation to any of the parties whose law he was replacing.

This was his greatest asset.

A practising English barrister sent to do the same job would have started from the common law. The result would have been a body of law intelligible primarily to English-trained lawyers, carrying centuries of common law baggage, and fundamentally organised around English social assumptions about property, family, and violence. Every session judge in Calcutta would have been comfortable. Every person the law was supposed to govern would have been mystified.

A practising Islamic jurist sent to the same task would have started from classical fiqh. The result would have been coherent for Muslim communities, entirely inapplicable to the Hindu population, and grounded in a theological framework that the British, as a matter of political survival, needed to remain neutral about.

What Macaulay did instead was to ask a question that a practitioner too invested in any existing system would not have thought to ask: what is a criminal code actually for? What does it need to do? What is the minimum information a person needs in order to know whether a given act is punishable, and by how much? And how do you write that down in a form that a person of ordinary intelligence can understand?

His answers, worked out in the Prefatory Address and in the structure of the Code itself, are still worth reading as a treatise on the philosophy of legal drafting. The Code should be precise. Precision matters more than elegance. Where precision and simplicity conflict, choose precision and add illustrations to explain it. The definitions and enacting clauses contain the whole law. The illustrations make nothing law that would not be law without them. They exist only to show the law in operation.

This last principle is more radical than it appears. In the common law tradition, decided cases accumulate over centuries and gradually redefine what the statute means — judges making law through interpretation in ways the original drafters never anticipated. Macaulay explicitly rejected this. He proposed that every time a court put a construction on a provision that the legislature had not intended, the legislature should resolve it by adding an illustration — a decided case made by the legislature, not the court, using the Code’s own drafting conventions, slotting into its proper place in the text.

“An addition of a very few pages to the Code will stand in the place of several volumes of reports,” he wrote, “and will be of far more value than such reports, inasmuch as the additions to the Code will proceed from the legislature, and will be of unquestionable authority; whereas the reports would only give the opinions of the judges, which other judges might venture to set aside.”

This is Benthamite codification at its most ambitious: law as a complete, self-contained, self-updating text, accessible to the governed rather than monopolised by the profession. The common law, with its centuries of reported cases and professional gatekeepers, was in Macaulay’s view a system that served lawyers more than it served justice.

Whether his ambition was fully realised is debatable. The common law’s interpretive traditions seeped into Pakistani courts regardless, through the judges trained in them. The Code has not been systematically updated through legislative illustration in the way Macaulay proposed. It has, instead, accumulated amendments, special laws, and judicial interpretations — becoming, in some respects, the thing he most wanted to avoid: a body of law that a professional needs to navigate and an ordinary person cannot read alone.

But the original text still bears the marks of his non-lawyerly clarity. The murder definition, the hurt gradation, the definition of dishonesty, the abetment framework: these are the work of a man who sat down with a blank page and asked what a murder is, what a theft is, what it means to help someone commit a crime — and wrote the answers down in language meant to be readable by the people it governed.

He was not a perfect draftsman. The Code has genuine gaps and inconsistencies, which the Law Commissioners of 1846 and 1847 were frank about in their First and Second Reports. Some provisions are over-complicated; some are under-specified. But the overall architecture — the General Part preceding the Special Part, the exceptions held in a single chapter applying to all offences, the uniform penalties graduated by harm and intent — is still the most coherent criminal code framework in the common-law world. England itself, the homeland of the common law, never managed to produce an equivalent.

The man who wasn’t a lawyer built the best criminal code his successors ever got.
reflect: Macaulay proposed that legal doubt should be resolved by legislative illustration rather than judicial precedent — that the Code should be constantly updated to incorporate how it is being applied. Pakistan has done the opposite: the text has stayed relatively stable while judicial interpretation has proliferated. Which approach is actually better for the person trying to understand whether a given act is an offence, and why?

The Code’s illustrations were meant to make the law accessible to the people it governs, not just to lawyers. How accessible is the current PPC to an educated Pakistani layperson who has not studied law? What would Macaulay think of the gap between his intention and the current reality?

title: One Law, Three Masters — The Sociological Arc from 1837 to Today
text:
In 1837, a British civil servant wrote a criminal code for a society he had studied for less than three years. In 1860, that code was enacted. In 1947, Pakistan came into being and inherited it wholesale. In 1979, a military government began grafting Islamic criminal law onto it. In 2024, Pakistani courts still apply the same basic framework — with the same section numbers, the same general exceptions, the same abetment and conspiracy provisions — that Macaulay signed off on in Calcutta 187 years ago.

Three political masters. One law. The question this essay asks is: why?

The first answer — the obvious one — is inertia. Legal systems are hard to change. Lawyers are trained in them, judges interpret them, the entire procedural apparatus is built around them. Starting from scratch would cost more than anyone was willing to pay. This is true, and it partially explains survival.

But inertia alone does not explain why the Code survived three transitions that were, in each case, genuinely revolutionary. The 1947 transition created a new nation with a different political identity and a different constitutional theology than the colonial state it replaced. That should have been an obvious moment to build a new criminal code — and in Pakistan’s early years there were serious arguments that it should happen. It didn’t. In 1979, Zia’s government was explicitly committed to Islamizing the legal order and had absolute power. If any government was capable of replacing the PPC entirely, it was his. He didn’t do that either. He amended. He grafted. He added. But the skeleton stayed.

The real explanation for survival is not inertia. It is that the PPC’s skeleton was built from social observations rather than political philosophy, and the social observations were accurate.

Macaulay spent three years looking at what actually happened in Indian society — what disputes people got into, how violence unfolded, what forms of property crime existed, how family structure created particular forms of coercion. The Code he produced reflects what he saw. Murder, theft, hurt, wrongful confinement, extortion, abetment, conspiracy — these are not categories derived from a philosophical theory of what crime should be. They are categories derived from what crime in 1837 British India actually was. And because the underlying social fabric — kinship-based, honour-driven, communal, agrarian — has changed more slowly than the political superstructure above it, the categories he identified remain recognisable today.

This is the deepest reason the Code survived 1947: not because Pakistan wanted to keep a British law, but because the law described Pakistani social reality as accurately as it had described Indian colonial reality. The honour killing provisions that the 2016 Criminal Law Amendment Act addressed were not about British values — they were about karo kari, a practice rooted in social dynamics older than the British presence in India. The laws against communal provocation, against false evidence, against bribery of public servants — these addressed problems present in 1837 and still present in 2024. The law survived because the problems it was designed for survived.

The 1979 Islamization tests this thesis at its most interesting point. What did Zia change, and why could he change it?

What he changed, primarily, was the punishment mechanism for the most serious offences against the person. He replaced the purely state-controlled punishment model with the victim-family-controlled model that classical Islamic jurisprudence requires: heirs of the victim decide whether to demand Qisas, accept Diyat, or forgive entirely. And he added the Hudood Ordinances, imposing hadd punishments for offences specifically addressed in the Quran.

What he could not change was the social fabric beneath the punishment mechanism. The family structures that Qisas assumes — heirs of the victim, male wali, blood money negotiations between families — were already present in Pakistani society. They had been present before Islam. The social institution of compensatory justice between families was not invented by Islamic law; Islamic law gave it a specific theological framework, but the institution itself is older. What Zia did, in one view, was to give an ancient social institution its correct theological name.

What he could not do — and did not try to do — was replace the Code’s fundamental logical architecture: the General Explanations, the actus reus and mens rea framework, the hierarchy of offences and exceptions, the structure of abetment and conspiracy. These are common-law inheritances baked too deeply into the professional and judicial culture of Pakistan’s courts to be easily replaced. Even the courts that administer Qisas and Diyat use the actus reus/mens rea framework to determine whether a qatl was committed, before applying the Islamic punishment to it. The vocabulary of Pakistani criminal courts — intention, knowledge, common intention, instigation, furtherance — is still the vocabulary of the 1860 Code.

The result is the hybrid entity described in Essay 1 of this series: a common-law skeleton wearing Islamic-law clothes in certain chapters. Neither pure. Both functional. Functional precisely because the social reality underneath — the family structures, the honour norms, the property disputes, the communal frictions — is coherent enough that both systems can address it, in their different vocabularies, without producing completely contradictory results.

What this arc teaches the student of Pakistani criminal law is something about how to read any provision you encounter. Every section of the PPC has a social history. Some sections address social facts so durable that they have survived three political transitions unchanged. Others were products of specific political moments and bear the marks of those moments — the post-1857 sedition provisions, the Zia-era amendments, the post-Qandeel Baloch 2016 reforms. Reading the law is not just reading the text. It is reading the society behind the text, and asking: what problem was this written to solve, and does that problem still exist?

Macaulay asked that question in 1837 and got most of the answers right. That is why you are still reading his work today.
reflect: Three political masters — colonial Britain, post-independence Pakistan, Zia’s Islamizing state — all kept the same basic Code with modifications rather than replacing it. Is this a sign that the Code was genuinely well-designed, or a sign that legal change is always harder than political change? Is there a difference between those two explanations?

The essay argues that provisions survive across political transitions when they address durable social facts. Identify one provision in the PPC that you think will not survive the next major political transition — one too closely tied to a specific political moment to endure — and explain your reasoning.

title: The Silence in the Law — Macaulay’s Silent Morality Principle
text:
There is something the Pakistan Penal Code never says. In 526 sections, across 26 chapters, covering everything from waging war against the state to using a false weight in the market, the Code never once explains why any of these things are wrong.

Not once.

S.302 does not say murder is wrong because God forbids it. It does not say murder is wrong because society has agreed to prohibit it. It does not say murder is wrong at all. It says: whoever commits qatl-i-amd shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term. The wrongness is assumed. The punishment is stated. The moral foundation is left entirely blank.

This is not an oversight. It is a design decision of the highest order, made by a man who understood precisely what he was doing and why it was necessary. Call it the Silent Morality Principle: the Code describes acts and consequences, and never says why the acts are wrong. The moral justification is deliberately, architecturally absent.

Consider what Macaulay would have faced if he had tried to include it. In 1837, governing a population that included practising Hindus, Muslims, Sikhs, Christians, Zoroastrians, and animists, any moral foundation he chose would have been someone else’s sectarian imposition. If he grounded the prohibition on murder in Islamic theology — “murder is forbidden because Allah forbids the taking of life without just cause” — he would have produced an Islamic law, and the Hindu population would have been governed by a code that declared their God’s authority irrelevant. If he grounded it in Hindu dharmic philosophy, he would have produced a Hindu code. If he grounded it in Christian natural law, he would have produced a colonial Christian code.

So he grounded it in nothing. He described the act. He specified the consequence. He left every reader to supply their own moral framework from within their own tradition, and in doing so he produced the only code all of them could accept without theological compromise.

The evidence of this design choice is everywhere in the 1837 draft, if you know how to look.

In Clause 69 of the 1837 draft — the consent exception to hurt — there is this illustration: “A converts Z, a person of ripe age and sound mind, to the Mahomedan religion, and, without any wilful misrepresentation, obtains Z’s consent to be circumcised. A circumcises Z. A has committed no offence.” Macaulay takes no position whatsoever on whether Islam is true, whether circumcision is theologically required, or whether converting to Islam is a good idea. He simply notes that an adult who freely consents to a religious rite that causes minor harm bears no legal grievance. The religious context is noted, bracketed, and left alone. The moral question — is this person’s religious conversion valid? — is none of the law’s business.

In Clause 283 of the 1837 draft, addressing the practice of Dhurna, a form of ancient Hindu coercive social pressure, the provision criminalises the act of “doing any act with the intention of causing it to be believed in any quarter that, by doing that act, he renders some other person an object of divine displeasure.” The illustration: “A sits Dhurna at Z’s door, with the intention of causing it to be believed that by so sitting he renders Z an object of divine displeasure.” The criminal act is using the threat of divine displeasure to coerce behaviour. The law does not say divine displeasure is not real. It does not say Dhurna is theologically invalid. It says: you cannot use supernatural threat as a tool of coercion against your neighbour. The theological question is left entirely open. Only the social consequence — coercion — is addressed.

In Clause 285, intentionally contaminating someone’s food against the rules of their religion or caste: “Whoever intentionally causes any food belonging to any person to be in a state in which that person, according to the rules of his religion or caste, cannot use it as food, shall be punished.” The law does not say caste is real. It does not say religious dietary rules are correct. It says: if you deliberately manipulate someone’s food to violate their religious practice, you have harmed them in a way the law recognises. The harm is the violation of autonomy and the social provocation it causes. The theology is left to the individual.

This is the Silent Morality Principle at work across three entirely different social contexts, in three entirely different legal chapters, producing the same structural result: the act is prohibited, the harm is acknowledged, and the moral foundation is left blank for each person to fill from their own tradition.

The practical consequence was a code that a Muslim could read and find consistent with Islamic values (for God also forbids murder, theft, and false testimony). A Hindu could read and find consistent with dharmic values. A secular rationalist could read and find consistent with utilitarian harm-prevention. None of them needed to agree on why. They all needed to agree on what.

Now ask: what happened in 1979?

Zia’s government filled in the blank. For the first time since 1860, the law said: murder is wrong, and the reason it is wrong — for this particular category of murder, committed by this particular kind of person against this particular kind of victim — is that Allah has prescribed Qisas. The punishment is no longer the state’s determination of social consequences. It is the Quran’s prescribed response to a category of prohibited act. The moral foundation is now Islamic theology.

This is not a small change dressed up in new vocabulary. It is a fundamental architectural change. The blank that Macaulay left — the deliberate, principled absence of moral foundation — was filled in. And once it is filled in for one category of offence, every subsequent debate about the law’s legitimacy takes place in theological terms, not legal ones. The question stops being: does this provision describe a real harm? It becomes: does this provision accord with correct Islamic teaching? Those are entirely different questions, and they require entirely different kinds of expertise to answer.

The irony is this: the very feature of the Code that made it acceptable to a Muslim majority in 1947 — its silence on moral foundation, which meant it made no anti-Islamic claims — was interpreted by 1979 as a deficiency to be corrected. The silence that enabled universal acceptance became the silence that Islamic law was brought in to fill.

Macaulay might have found this unsurprising. He understood that legal neutrality is unstable — that a law which refuses to state its moral foundation will eventually be claimed by whoever has the political power to claim it. The silence was a holding position, not a permanent settlement. It held for 119 years. That is either a remarkable achievement or a delayed failure, depending on what you think a criminal code’s moral foundation should be.
reflect: The Silent Morality Principle held for 119 years before Zia’s intervention broke it. Is a law that never states why its prohibitions are wrong more or less legitimate than a law that grounds itself in a specific moral tradition? Does a criminal code need a stated moral foundation to function, or does functional consensus on prohibited acts make the foundational question irrelevant?

Every provision of the PPC prohibits something. Pick one provision from any module you have studied and ask: what moral foundation would a Muslim jurist supply for this prohibition? What moral foundation would a secular utilitarian supply? Are the foundations compatible? Does it matter if they are not?

title: The Calculus of Pain — Bentham, Macaulay, and the Architecture of Punishment
text:
RH11 — The Calculus of Pain: Bentham, Macaulay, and the Architecture of Punishment

Jeremy Bentham began with a proposition so simple it sounds almost naive: the only legitimate purpose of law is to produce the greatest happiness for the greatest number. Pleasure and pain are real, measurable, and comparable across persons. The legislator's job is not to enforce divine command, not to express moral outrage, not to give victims satisfaction — it is to do arithmetic. This is the felicific calculus, and once you accept it, everything that follows from it is almost mechanically inevitable.

Punishment, in this framework, is by definition an evil. It causes pain. The only justification for inflicting it is that it prevents a greater pain — the harm the offence causes to society. This means every punishment the law imposes must be the minimum necessary to outweigh the pleasure the offender gains from the crime. Not a rupee more, not a day longer. Over-punishment is not justice. It is waste — the state inflicting pain that produces no corresponding social benefit, which in Bentham's calculus is simply irrational.

From that single premise, four requirements follow. Punishment must be proportionate, scaled to the severity of the harm. It must be certain, because a small but inevitable consequence deters more effectively than a large but uncertain one. It must be swift, because the human mind connects punishment to conduct only when the two are close in time. And wherever possible, it must be reversible — because a system that cannot correct its own errors is a system that will eventually execute the innocent with no remedy.

Now ask which instrument of punishment satisfies all four requirements simultaneously, across an infinite range of offence severity, for every class of offender regardless of wealth.

The answer is imprisonment. A year is a year. It is proportionate — you can give one month or twenty-five years. It is measurable — the offender and society both know exactly what is being applied. It is reversible — a wrongful conviction can be corrected and time partially compensated. And it converts every offence into the same currency, which makes the entire punishment schedule internally consistent and comparable. When you look at the Pakistan Penal Code and find imprisonment dominating more than 260 of its provisions, you are not looking at historical accident. You are looking at Benthamite logic applied systematically to an entire criminal code.

Death fails every test except finality. It cannot be graduated — you cannot give someone half a death for a half-serious murder. It cannot be reversed. It cannot be calibrated to fit the infinite gradations between a premeditated killing and a reckless one. And it defeats the equalization logic entirely, since a rich man and a poor man both die the same death, but only a rich man can purchase conditions that make imprisonment something other than imprisonment. Fine fails differently — it is scalable but not equal, hitting the poor man catastrophically and the wealthy man negligibly. Transportation was the era's compromise — removal from society, scalable by duration, reversible in theory. Imprisonment replaced it because it was cheaper, more certain, and more uniformly applicable.

Here a separate philosophical tradition arrives at the same destination by a completely different road, and this convergence is not accidental.

The natural rights tradition running from Locke through the American Declaration of Independence and the French Declaration of the Rights of Man established a hierarchy of what the state may take from a citizen: property first, then liberty, then life, in ascending order of inviolability. These rights are not granted by the state — they precede it. The state exists to protect them, and may infringe them only under the most pressing justification.

Map that hierarchy onto the PPC's punishment ladder. The smallest offences attract fines — the state takes your property. More serious offences attract imprisonment — the state takes your liberty. The most grave attract death — the state takes your life. The two hierarchies mirror each other inversely: the least important right is touched first, the most important last. Bentham, who famously called natural rights "nonsense upon stilts," would have been irritated to find his utilitarian punishment schedule perfectly replicating the Lockean rights hierarchy. But he would have understood why: both frameworks, starting from entirely different premises, were tracking the same underlying human intuition about the relative value of property, freedom, and life. When two philosophies in fundamental disagreement produce identical practical conclusions, that convergence is evidence that both have identified something real.

The Romans who preceded both traditions had no such convergence. Before the Twelve Tables, Roman law operated almost entirely as private wrong — the injured party pursued their own remedy, and the state was largely absent from the transaction. The punishment ladder that emerged from that system ran: fine, imprisonment, mutilation, death. Mutilation sat in the middle, between confinement and killing, as a standard intermediate instrument rather than an extreme. And in the Roman world, as in the pre-Islamic Arabian world, wealth determined outcomes almost entirely — the powerful family negotiated blood-price down to a token, the weak family accepted it or risked a feud they could not win.

Macaulay looked at that history and built something deliberately different. His Code contains no mutilation. No lashes. No bodily punishment between fine and imprisonment. The jump from liberty to life is large and is meant to be large — the space between them is filled entirely by gradations of imprisonment, from days to life. This was not squeamishness. It was Benthamite precision: mutilation is irreversible and therefore irrational; it cannot be calibrated to offence severity; it serves no deterrent function that imprisonment cannot serve better; and it produces permanent harm that society must then sustain. Every argument against it is an argument from the calculus.

The Code that emerged from these principles was, as Macaulay's Prefatory Address makes clear, an instrument of governance. Its declared object was public tranquility — the maintenance of order among a diverse, multi-religious, multi-ethnic population living around shared infrastructure, contested sacred spaces, and deep communal frictions. It was not designed to liberate the widow burned on her husband's pyre, or the woman confined by her family, or the child married before she could consent. These people appear in the Code — sati is addressed, wrongful confinement is punished, consent is defined. But the Code reaches them only when someone invokes it on their behalf, and the law assumes a functioning enforcement apparatus and a population with enough social standing to access its protections. Where those conditions do not exist, the law is present on paper and absent from life.

This was not unique to Macaulay or to British India. Every criminal code in history has been an instrument of order before it has been an instrument of justice. What distinguished the 1837 Code was the sophistication and internal consistency of the order it tried to impose — the rational, scalable, equalized punishment architecture that Bentham's philosophy made possible.

Then 1979 arrived, and the architecture broke — not once but twice simultaneously.

The first break was instrumental. Lashes entered a punishment system that had no place for them. Amputation entered for specific categories of theft under the Hudood Ordinances. These are not Benthamite instruments. They are irreversible. They cannot be graduated — you cannot give someone half a lash for a half-serious offence. They do not convert into the common currency of time that makes imprisonment internally consistent. And they reintroduce bodily punishment that Macaulay had deliberately excluded.

The second break was deeper. It was foundational. Bentham's system punishes because punishment prevents greater harm — the justification is consequentialist and secular. The Hudood framework punishes because Allah has commanded it — the justification is theological and non-negotiable. A judge applying Benthamite logic can reduce a sentence if the deterrent effect has already been achieved, if the offender poses no further risk, if rehabilitation is possible. A judge applying hadd cannot reduce the prescribed punishment at all, because the punishment is not his to modify. Divine command is not subject to the felicific calculus.

The two frameworks do not translate into each other. They answer the question "why punish?" with answers so different that placing them in the same statute book creates not a synthesis but a collision — one chapter reasoning in utilitarian arithmetic, the next reasoning in theological certainty, both purporting to be criminal law.

Daman — the open-ended compensation instrument without floor or ceiling — sits at the point where the two traditions almost touch. Its purpose, as classical Islamic jurisprudence conceived it, was precisely to prevent wealth from purchasing exit from consequence. No fixed amount means no predictable price means no transaction that power can control in advance. In that specific function, Daman and Bentham are pursuing the same enemy: the wealthy offender who converts the law's remedy into a manageable cost of doing business.

Which brings us to the question the Code cannot answer by itself.

Imprisonment is theoretically the great equalizer. When everyone is behind bars, it is the same thing — or it should be. But in contemporary Pakistan, the rich prisoner purchases air conditioning, a television, a mobile phone, fast food delivered from outside. The poor prisoner receives what the state can afford to provide, which is the essential minimum. The conditions diverge so dramatically that two people serving identical sentences for identical offences are experiencing punishment so different it is almost categorically distinct.

Macaulay gave the Code two tiers: rigorous imprisonment, which included hard labor, and simple imprisonment, which was confinement without it. In 1837 that distinction was operationally meaningful. In contemporary Pakistan it has largely collapsed as a legal concept, replaced by an entirely extra-legal stratification based on what the prisoner can afford to pay for inside the facility.

Bentham's equalization logic requires uniform conditions varied only by duration. One tier of experience, infinite gradation of time — that is the only version of the system that preserves both the equality principle and the proportionality principle simultaneously. What Pakistan has instead is informal, corrupt, and entirely invisible to the formal punishment schedule: the same sentence meaning radically different things depending on your bank balance.

The Code is Benthamite on paper. In practice it is something the 1837 draft never anticipated: a system where the philosophical architecture is sound, the statutory text is coherent, and the actual experience of punishment varies by wealth in ways the law neither acknowledges nor corrects.

Bentham would have called that irrational. He would have been right. Whether any criminal code, anywhere, has fully solved it is a different question — and an honest answer requires admitting that no system yet has.
reflect: Bentham called punishment an evil — justified only when it prevents a greater evil. Does that framing change how you read the PPC’s punishment schedule? If every sentence is an admission that the state is doing something regrettable, does that impose any obligation on how sentences should be designed?

The rights hierarchy (property → liberty → life) and the punishment hierarchy (fine → imprisonment → death) mirror each other inversely — two traditions arriving at the same structure from opposite starting points. Is that convergence evidence of good design, or simply evidence that both traditions were tracking the same basic human intuitions about what matters?

Imprisonment is theoretically the great equaliser — a year is a year regardless of wealth. But in Pakistani prisons, conditions diverge dramatically based on what a prisoner can afford to pay for inside the facility. If that gap is permanent and uncorrectable, has the Benthamite system actually been implemented in Pakistan at all?

title: The Law That Assumed Women — From Macaulay’s Silences to Justice Malik’s Dissent
text:
Macaulay drafted the 1860 Code for a society he had spent three years observing. What he saw, with considerable clarity, was a legal landscape in which women had no independent standing — no right to sue, to own property in their own name, to contract, or to appear before a court without a male intermediary. He did not design the Code to change this. He designed the Code to govern it. The silences in the 1860 text are not oversights. They are architectural decisions made by a man who understood exactly what kind of society he was legislating for.

The word "consent" appears in the Code, but the law defines it negatively — by its absence. Fear, intoxication, unsoundness of mind: these are the conditions under which consent is vitiated. The Code never defines what consent positively is. It never asks whether a woman freely, consciously, and voluntarily agreed to anything. It simply lists the circumstances under which her agreement does not count. This is not carelessness. It reflects a society in which women's autonomous agreement was not legally relevant in the first place. A father's decision about his daughter's marriage was not consent obtained from her — it was consent given on her behalf. The daughter's silence was treated as agreement. The law had no mechanism to distinguish the two, because in 1860 the law did not need one.

This is the Code's deepest structural assumption: that women exist within family structures which represent their interests. A woman wrongfully confined could theoretically invoke S.340. A woman coerced could invoke the duress provisions of the General Exceptions. But both provisions assume someone with standing to approach the law, the social capacity to be heard when they do, and a family structure disposed to help rather than to harm. Where those conditions did not hold — and for most women in British India they did not hold — the law was present on paper and absent in practice.

That original silence set in motion a pattern that has repeated itself across 165 years of Pakistani legal history. Each generation inherits the silence. Eventually a case arrives that makes the silence visible. A landmark judgment or a legislative response partially fills the gap. And then the next silence is revealed.

The first major rupture came not from the original Code but from what was grafted onto it. The Zina Ordinance of 1979 did not create the problem of women's legal vulnerability — it created a new, specifically modern version of it, one that the 1860 Code had not produced. By fusing zina-bil-jabr (rape) with zina (consensual fornication) under a single evidentiary standard, and importing the hadd proof requirement of four male witnesses into rape prosecution, the Ordinance created a structural trap that the original Code had not contained. Macaulay's S.375 had defined rape without importing Islamic evidentiary standards. The 1979 Ordinance collapsed that distinction entirely, and the consequences were immediate.

Safia Bibi's case, tried in Sahiwal in 1983, showed the trap in its purest form. A nearly blind teenage domestic worker, raped by her employer Muhammad Ali and his son Maqsood Ahmed, was unable to identify her attackers visually or produce four male witnesses to the act. The rapists were acquitted the same day. She was convicted of zina — her own pregnancy treated as evidence against her — and sentenced to fifteen lashes, three years imprisonment, and a fine. The Federal Shariat Court overturned the conviction on appeal. But the structural logic that produced it remained unchanged. The Women's Action Forum took to the streets. International attention followed. And yet the Women's Protection Act that partially reversed the Ordinance did not arrive until 2006 — twenty-three years after Safia Bibi's conviction. That gap is not incidental. It is the law's standard lag time behind social reality.

The Diyat system opened a different category of gap, visible in two cases from opposite ends of the social spectrum that are structurally identical. In 2011, CIA contractor Raymond Davis shot and killed two Pakistani men in Lahore. The families signed forgiveness affidavits and received approximately $2.3 million in Diyat. Davis was acquitted and flown out of Pakistan within hours. The families' own lawyer stated publicly that they had been coerced. The classical mechanism for private reconciliation between genuinely grieving families was being operated as a geopolitical instrument by a foreign intelligence service. Diyat's waivability — designed to give bereaved families a meaningful choice — had been converted into a purchasing mechanism for state power.

Five years later, Qandeel Baloch was strangled by her brother Muhammad Waseem in their family home in Multan, killed for the social media posts he considered dishonourable. The family forgave him under the same Diyat provisions. The brother who committed the killing was part of the wali network whose forgiveness extinguished the claim. This was the pre-2016 wali loophole in its most extreme form: the killer and the forgiver were the same family. The Criminal Law Amendment Act 2016 amended S.311 specifically to address this — making honour killings non-compoundable through family forgiveness, mandating a minimum sentence regardless of wali consent. One woman had to die for the gap to become visible enough to close.

Raymond Davis and Qandeel Baloch represent the same structural failure from opposite ends. Both demonstrate Diyat's waivability being captured by the very party that should have faced accountability. The 2016 amendment closed the honour killing variant. The state power variant remains structurally unaddressed.

Between these two arcs runs a third, quieter story that reveals the deepest thing about how law and social change interact in Pakistan.

Saima Waheed married without her father's consent in 1996. Her father, Hafiz Abdul Waheed Ropri, a man of significant religious standing in Lahore, challenged the marriage. The Lahore High Court initially ruled against her — holding that a Muslim woman could not validly enter a nikah without her wali's consent. The case wound through the courts for years. The Supreme Court finally ruled in 2003 that an adult Muslim woman can enter a valid nikah of her own free will without guardian consent. Asma Jahangir defended her throughout.

The Saima Waheed case is typically read as a marriage law case. It is also a consent case. The legal question — can a woman's independent consent constitute a valid marriage without her guardian's co-signature — is structurally identical to the question running through every gap this essay has traced: whose autonomous agreement counts under Pakistani law? Macaulay's Code had no answer because it assumed the question would not arise. In 1837, the answer was obvious: a woman's consent was mediated through her family. By 1996, urban middle-class Pakistan had changed enough that the question had become litigable. A woman could exist with enough independence to contract a marriage her father opposed, sustain seven years of legal proceedings, and eventually prevail.

That shift — from women as legal non-entities mediated entirely through family structure, to women as individuals capable of exercising legal rights directly — is the most fundamental social change the Code has had to absorb since 1860. And it did not happen uniformly, suddenly, or completely. It is happening now, unevenly, concentrated in urban centres and among the educated middle class.

Consider the arc over three generations. Sixty years ago, a woman attending school was a source of stigma in much of Pakistan. Forty years ago, a woman working outside the home was widely considered to be of questionable character regardless of her profession. Today, women constitute close to half of new entrants into the formal job market in certain urban sectors. That is not simply a social achievement — it is a legal stress test. Every new space women occupy reveals the gaps the law left when it assumed they would not be there.

The Protection Against Harassment of Women at the Workplace Act 2010 is the direct legislative response to exactly this shift. The Act was necessary only because women were present in formal workplaces in sufficient numbers to encounter harassment as a systemic pattern rather than an isolated private misfortune. The 1860 Code's existing provisions — S.354 covering assault with intent to outrage modesty, S.509 covering verbal and gestural insult — were calibrated for a social world where harassment was expected to manifest as overt physical force or explicit verbal abuse. They are structurally inadequate for formal employment environments, where harassment operates through power dynamics, professional dependency, and the implicit threat embedded in hierarchy. The 2010 Act was not a correction of Macaulay's drafting. It was a response to a social reality he had no reason to anticipate.

The gaps within that shift are still opening. Pakistan has no specific criminal provision for groping — non-violent sexual touching in public or professional spaces. S.354's "assault or criminal force with intent to outrage modesty" technically reaches it, but requires proof of intent, carries a maximum of two years, and has been historically applied to severe physical force rather than routine, quotidian contact. The Law and Justice Commission of Pakistan has itself noted the absence and proposed a specific molestation provision. The gap is known. It has not been closed.

In November 2020, a credit department employee at Faysal Bank's F-10 branch in Islamabad — Usman Gohar — was recorded on camera groping a female colleague as she stood by his desk. He sat back down as if nothing had happened. A colleague had filmed it. The video went viral the same day. By evening, the DC of Islamabad had taken notice, police had conducted raids, the bank had terminated Gohar, and by that night he had been arrested. The State Bank barred him from employment in any Pakistani bank.

Notice what the video made possible that the law alone could not. Without it, the incident would most likely have been absorbed in silence — by the victim, by her colleagues, by the institution. The victim had shown no visible resistance. She had stood still, said nothing. And the immediate social media reaction, alongside the outrage, included a significant strand of victim-blaming asking why she had not reacted — exactly the reasoning the Supreme Court majority would use four years later in Muhammad Imran v. The State to infer consent from a rape victim's silence and absence of physical resistance. The same analytical failure, operating simultaneously in public discourse and in the highest court of the land.

More recently, a man was filmed committing exhibitionism in Islamabad, the video circulating on social media within hours. Public outcry followed immediately. These incidents — groping, exhibitionism, workplace harassment captured on mobile phones — are not new offences. They were happening before social media existed. What social media has done is make them undeniable. A woman who reported groping before the era of smartphone cameras had only her own testimony, facing all the structural skepticism the courts applied to women's accounts of sexual misconduct. Now the act is sometimes on record. The evidence exists. And what the evidence consistently reveals is that the law's response infrastructure — its definitions, its proof requirements, its sentencing — was built for a world where these acts were either invisible or unprovable.

Which brings the essay to its sharpest point, and the most recent landmarks on this arc.

In 2024, the Supreme Court of Pakistan delivered its judgment in Muhammad Imran v. The State (2024 SCP 226). The facts were unambiguous: a woman had reported rape; DNA evidence conclusively matched the accused; two witnesses had heard her cry out; both the trial court and the Lahore High Court had convicted. The Supreme Court majority — two judges to one — converted the rape conviction to a charge of consensual fornication under S.496-B. Their reasoning: no physical marks of struggle on the victim's body. A two-day delay in filing the FIR. A prior familiarity between the accused and the complainant.
