Enforced disappearance by state actors is a different category of failure. It is not a gap in reach. It is a gap in the direction the law is designed to move.

The PPC was designed to regulate the conduct of private actors, with the state as the prosecuting authority and the victim or their family as the complainant. The system assumes that the state's interests and the victim's interests point in the same direction — both want the private wrongdoer identified and punished. In enforced disappearance cases, those interests have inverted. The state or state-adjacent actor is the alleged wrongdoer. The prosecution apparatus, the investigation machinery, the courts themselves, and the commissions established to investigate all operate within the same institutional structure as the alleged perpetrator. The system is being asked to prosecute itself.

In December 2024, a six-judge Constitutional Bench of the Supreme Court reaffirmed that only Parliament has the authority to address enforced disappearances. Justice Jamal Khan Mandokhail observed: "The court has always recognised Parliament as the supreme body and now it is for Parliament to prove it so." Parliament had introduced the Criminal Laws Amendment Act 2023. Parliament had not, as of that date, enacted specific legislation criminalising enforced disappearance by named state actors, establishing independent investigative authority with compulsory process against security agencies, or creating a prosecutorial mechanism capable of operating outside the ordinary chain of command.

The court deferred to Parliament. Parliament deferred to consensus among stakeholders. The stakeholders include the security establishment. The security establishment has not consented to accountability. The circle is closed from inside by the institution that would have to open it.

WHEN ACCOUNTABILITY HAS COME ELSEWHERE

The honest historical record is that enforced disappearance by state actors has been prosecuted successfully in very few places — and in each case, accountability followed political change rather than leading it.

Argentina prosecuted its military junta's disappearances after the junta fell in 1983. The trials were not possible while the regime existed; they were possible because the regime no longer existed. Chile prosecuted Pinochet-era disappearances after the return of democracy and after years of civil society pressure that slowly eroded the legal protections the transition settlement had attempted to preserve for military officers. South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission addressed apartheid-era disappearances after apartheid ended — and even then chose a model of conditional amnesty over prosecution, because full prosecution was judged politically unsustainable.

In each case, the sequence was the same: political conditions changed first, accountability followed. The law did not produce the political change. The political change made the law available as an accountability instrument.

This is not a counsel of despair about Pakistani law. It is an observation about the limits of what any law can achieve when the institution the law is meant to constrain controls the conditions under which the law is applied. The mother outside the Quetta Press Club is not waiting for a better-drafted statute. She is waiting for the political conditions that would make the statute that already exists applicable to the people who took her son.

THE OPEN QUESTION

The Criminal Laws Amendment Act 2023 is, in one reading, a genuine legislative achievement: Parliament acknowledged that private jails exist, named the conduct, defined the perpetrators, and attached criminal consequences. The law that was previously silent on this specific form of confinement is silent no longer.

In another reading, it is the latest iteration of the pattern RH13 identified: a layer added on top of existing law, drafted to address a genuine harm, containing a provision broad enough to theoretically reach the perpetrators most urgently in need of accountability, and in practice applied only to the perpetrators least able to resist it — the feudal landlord in rural Sindh, the Jirga head in Balochistan — while the state actors who account for the largest share of the Commission's 10,467 cases continue operating without consequence.

Both readings are accurate. They are not in conflict. The law can be simultaneously a real step forward and an insufficient one. It can name the thing without reaching the people who most need to be named. It can create a criminal provision that no prosecutor will use against the perpetrators it was most urgently designed to address.

The QSO's seven-year presumption, the Commission's endless monthly reports, the Supreme Court's deference to Parliament, and Parliament's 2023 amendment that reached everything except the thing it most needed to reach — taken together, these are not a story of legal failure in the ordinary sense. They are a story of what happens when the law is asked to provide accountability for conduct committed by the institution that controls the conditions of legal accountability.

What that requires is not a better statute. It requires the political conditions under which an existing statute becomes enforceable against the people who currently stand above its reach. The law has an answer. The answer is not yet available. The distance between those two sentences is not a legal distance. It is a political one — and that is the distance the mother outside the Quetta Press Club has been measuring, in days, for fifteen years.
reflect: The QSO’s seven-year presumption of death resolves the civil question — inheritance, remarriage, estate — without touching the criminal question at all. Is this the right trade-off? Should a legal system provide civil resolution before criminal accountability is established, or does doing so reduce the pressure on the state to ever establish accountability? What assumptions about the relationship between civil and criminal law does your answer reveal?

The Criminal Laws Amendment Act 2023 defines ‘influential person’ broadly enough to theoretically include state actors, but has not produced a single documented prosecution of a state actor in the years since its passage. Is the failure of enforcement a drafting problem, a political problem, or an institutional design problem? What would a statute specifically designed to be enforceable against state actors — not just in principle but in practice — look like differently?

Argentina, Chile, and South Africa all achieved meaningful accountability for state-perpetrated disappearances only after political transition made prosecution possible. The law followed political change; it did not produce it. Does this mean Pakistan must wait for a comparable political transition before the Criminal Laws Amendment Act’s provisions become enforceable against state actors? Or is there a mechanism — international courts, treaty obligations, domestic judicial activism — that could create accountability without waiting for the political conditions to shift first?

title: The Mind the Law Cannot Read — Mens Rea, McNaughton, and the Inference Trap
text:
RH18 — The Mind the Law Cannot Read: Mens Rea, McNaughton, and the Inference Trap

Every criminal conviction in the Pakistan Penal Code requires the court to make a finding about something it cannot directly observe: what was happening inside the accused's mind at the moment of the act. The actus reus — the physical act itself — is visible, recoverable, provable through witnesses and forensic evidence. The mens rea — the criminal intention or knowledge that transforms a physical act into a crime — exists only in a mind that the court cannot enter, in a moment that has already passed, and must be reconstructed from evidence that is always indirect and often contested.

This is not a gap in the Code. It is a foundational feature of criminal law as a discipline. Every serious legal system faces it. The question is not whether the law must make inferences about mental states — it must, always — but whether the tools it uses to make those inferences are reliable enough to justify the consequences they produce. In the Pakistan Penal Code, three provisions expose the answer with particular clarity: Section 84's insanity defence, Section 120-A's conspiracy offence, and Section 34's common intention doctrine. Each approaches the mind differently. Each fails differently. And taken together, they reveal something about the law that its neutral statutory language carefully conceals.

THE TEST THAT CANNOT SEE WHAT IT NEEDS TO SEE

Section 84 of the PPC provides that nothing is an offence done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that what he is doing is either wrong or contrary to law. The provision codifies the principles established in 1843 when the English House of Lords, responding to the acquittal of Daniel McNaughton on grounds of insanity, formulated a set of questions that became the standard test for criminal insanity across common law jurisdictions. Pakistan inherited the test. It has retained it.

The McNaughton test asks a cognitive question: did the accused know the nature of the act, or know that it was wrong? It does not ask a volitional question: was the accused capable of stopping themselves even if they knew? This is the test's first and most fundamental limitation. A person experiencing a seizure, a dissociative episode, or command hallucinations may know exactly what they are doing and be entirely unable to stop. They satisfy the cognitive test — they know the nature of the act — and therefore receive no protection under S.84, despite the fact that their conduct was not in any meaningful sense a product of choice.

McNaughton was designed for a different kind of case: the chronic, stable, observable delusion that permanently removes the accused from legal reality, as McNaughton himself experienced. The test has no framework for conditions that are intermittent, situationally triggered, or episodic — conditions that leave no visible trace between episodes and may last only seconds or minutes at the moment of the act. For these conditions, the test produces two kinds of failure simultaneously.

The first is the false negative: the genuinely ill person who cannot be protected. A person who has never been formally diagnosed, never hospitalised, never entered the psychiatric system, suffers a genuine episode and commits an act they would not otherwise have committed. How does the court establish that the episode occurred? The witnesses are the victim and, if anyone, bystanders who observed behaviour, not internal mental state. The medical evidence, if any, is gathered after the fact. Prior psychiatric history — the most persuasive evidence available — does not exist. The defence under S.84 is practically unavailable to people who were never in the psychiatric system before the offence, regardless of their actual mental state at the moment of the act. The protection the section offers is not distributed according to clinical reality. It is distributed according to prior access to healthcare.

The second is the false positive: the manufactured defence that the test cannot reliably detect. A 1996 American film, Primal Fear, dramatised this failure with precision: a defendant successfully runs a multiple personality disorder defence, securing acquittal from a jury convinced by his apparent symptoms — and reveals at the end that the disorder was entirely fabricated. The film is fiction. The legal vulnerability it depicts is not. McNaughton's test relies on the court's ability to assess a mental state it cannot directly observe, using evidence that a sophisticated actor can replicate. The same evidentiary gap that makes genuine episodic insanity impossible to prove also makes fabricated insanity difficult to disprove. The test is vulnerable from both directions simultaneously — too rigid for genuine episodic conditions, too credulous for calculated performance.

The volitional gap is the test's deepest failure. McNaughton asks: did you know? It does not ask: could you stop? These are different questions, and for a significant range of psychiatric and neurological conditions they produce different answers. A person who knows exactly what they are doing but is biologically incapable of interrupting the conduct — the compulsion, the seizure, the dissociative automatism — is sane under McNaughton and criminally liable under S.84. The law is, in those cases, punishing a biological event rather than a moral choice. Whether punishment serves any of its stated purposes — deterrence, retribution, rehabilitation — in relation to conduct that was not chosen is a question the Code does not ask because the McNaughton framework does not require it to.

THE OFFENCE THAT PUNISHES BEFORE THE ACT

Section 120-A defines criminal conspiracy as an agreement between two or more persons to do or cause to be done an illegal act, or a legal act by illegal means. The offence is complete at the moment of agreement. No preparation is required. No attempt. No commission. The agreement itself is the actus reus, and the intention to pursue the agreed illegal object is the mens rea. Criminal conspiracy is the law's furthest reach into the future — it attaches liability to a mental state before any physical manifestation of that state has occurred.

This is a deliberate and coherent policy choice, as RH5 traced. Society has a legitimate interest in preventing dangerous conduct before it produces its intended harm. An agreement to commit murder is dangerous before any step toward murder has been taken, because the agreement creates a social fact — an organised criminal intent — that is more likely to produce the harm than any individual's uncoordinated intention. The law intervenes at the agreement because waiting for the attempt would mean waiting until the danger is far more difficult to prevent.

The problem is evidentiary. An agreement exists in minds. It leaves no physical trace unless it is recorded, documented, or acknowledged. The vast majority of criminal conspiracy prosecutions — in Pakistan and everywhere else — proceed without direct evidence of the agreement itself. They proceed on inference: from the fact that the accused knew each other, from their shared ideology or organisational membership, from their presence at the same places at the same times, from conduct that the prosecution argues could only have been coordinated. The inference is presented as proof of the agreement. The jury or court is asked to conclude that the agreement must have existed because the conduct is otherwise inexplicable.

This inference is logically fragile in a way the law rarely acknowledges. Two people independently radicalised by the same ideology, consuming the same material, arriving at the same conclusions through separate processes, can produce conduct indistinguishable from the conduct of two people who explicitly agreed. The law distinguishes them by the inference of agreement — but the inference is drawn from the same conduct in both cases. What makes it an agreement in one case and independent parallel action in another is a judgment call that rests on something other than the conduct itself.

What it rests on, in practice, is the prior profile of the accused. And this is where the conspiracy provision's evidentiary structure connects to something the Code's neutral language obscures.

THE PROFILE AS THE REAL TRIGGER

Pakistan's major conspiracy prosecutions do not follow a pattern in which the state discovers an agreement and then identifies the conspirators. They follow the reverse pattern: the state identifies people it already considers dangerous and constructs the conspiracy prosecution around them. The Rawalpindi Conspiracy Case of 1951 — in which Major General Akbar Khan and a group of army officers and Communist Party members were prosecuted for allegedly planning to overthrow the government — proceeded on evidence that was largely inference from political associations, shared ideological sympathies, and attendance at common meetings. There was no recorded agreement. The prosecution worked because the state already had these people under surveillance, already considered them threats, already had files on their prior activities. The conspiracy charge provided the legal vehicle. The profile provided the substance.

The mechanism operates identically in contemporary Pakistan, and with the entire media apparatus as witness. During the 2014 sit-in protest against alleged election rigging — which ran for over 100 days — Imran Khan delivered nightly speeches to the crowd assembled in Islamabad. In one of those late-night addresses, he used a cricket metaphor: he said he was waiting for the umpire's finger. In cricket, the umpire raising the finger signals that a batsman is out — the decisive moment has arrived. Khan was saying he was waiting for a signal that the moment to escalate had come.

The phrase was immediately interpreted by political opponents and sections of the media as a coded communication to the military: act now, remove the government. Whether that was his intent is unknowable. Whether it constituted any legally cognisable act is a separate question entirely. For an ordinary citizen, that sentence is a cricket metaphor. It carries no legal weight. No conspiracy inference attaches to it. No surveillance file opens on it.

For Imran Khan in 2014 — already in the state's field of attention, already framed as a destabilising actor, already under the interpretive lens that comes with high-visibility political opposition — the same sentence became evidence of something. Not because the sentence changed. Because the speaker's profile changed what the sentence meant to the people watching it. The phrase did not establish a conspiracy. But for a person already in the crosshairs, it became a data point in a file being assembled with a conclusion already in mind. Every subsequent prosecution of Khan — and there have been over 150 cases filed against him since 2022, including under the Official Secrets Act and anti-terrorism provisions — drew on exactly this accumulated interpretive record. The umpire's finger, the speeches, the rallies: all of it entered the profile. The profile preceded every charge. The charges gave the profile legal form.

This pattern recurs across Pakistani legal history with remarkable consistency. Conspiracy prosecutions cluster around political opponents of the ruling establishment, religious organisations the state has targeted for suppression, and ethnic separatist movements in Balochistan and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. The ordinary citizen — invisible to the state's surveillance apparatus, with no prior file, no documented associations, no history of political activity the state considers threatening — is practically immune to conspiracy prosecution not because S.120-A's language excludes them but because the evidentiary infrastructure for building a conspiracy case depends on prior suspicion, and prior suspicion depends on prior visibility.

The provision presents itself as neutral: it applies to any agreement to do an illegal act, regardless of who the agreers are. Its application is anything but neutral. The trigger for prosecution is not the agreement — which is almost never directly observable — but the profile of the people alleged to have made it. The agreement is reconstructed backward from the profile. Prior speeches, associations, organisational memberships, and surveillance records become the evidence of the agreement rather than evidence that an agreement was separately established and then confirmed through those associations.

This means S.120-A operates as what might be called a profile-activated weapon: dormant against the invisible ordinary citizen, available against the visible political or ideological actor. The invisibility of the ordinary citizen is not a legal protection the Code provides. It is an accidental protection produced by the state's limited attention — and it disappears the moment the state decides to look.

THE TRAP OF PRESENCE

Section 34 provides that when a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of them is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone. Common intention makes each participant fully liable for the act of any one of them, provided they shared the intention in furtherance of which the act was done.

Common intention is, again, a mental state. It must be proved, not presumed. The courts have consistently held that it cannot be established merely from presence — mere presence at the scene does not establish common intention to commit the act committed. But in practice, the inference from presence to common intention is one of the most frequently drawn inferences in Pakistani criminal jurisprudence, particularly in cases involving multiple accused from the same family, community, or organisation.

The structural problem is that common intention must pre-exist the act — it cannot be formed at the moment of the act itself — but the evidence for its pre-existence is almost always gathered after the act. The court is asked to infer that the accused shared an intention before the act based on what they did during and after it. This backward inference from conduct to prior mental state is exactly the same logic as the conspiracy inference, applied at a different stage of the analysis.

And exactly the same profile-dependency operates. Common intention is most readily inferred when the accused already appear to the court as a coherent dangerous group — when their relationships, backgrounds, and prior conduct make the inference of shared planning plausible without requiring specific evidence of the plan. Five strangers present at a scene are harder to convict under S.34 than five members of the same family, the same gang, or the same political organisation, because the inference of common intention is easier to draw when the prior relationship is visible. The law demands proof of a mental state. The court finds that proof in the profile.

WHAT THREE PROVISIONS REVEAL

McNaughton, S.120-A, and S.34 approach the mind from different directions and attach different consequences to what they find. But they share a structural feature that their neutral statutory language does not disclose: all three give the state enormous discretion in deciding whose mental state to scrutinise and when, and all three produce outcomes that depend as much on the prior visibility of the accused to the state as on the facts of the specific case.

McNaughton's protection is practically available only to those with prior psychiatric records — which correlates with resources, access to healthcare, and prior engagement with formal institutional systems. Conspiracy liability under S.120-A is practically activated primarily against those with prior political or ideological profiles. Common intention under S.34 is most readily inferred against those whose prior relationships make the inference easy to draw.

The law presents these as provisions about mental states, neutrally applied to all minds equally. The operational reality is that they apply most powerfully to minds the state has already decided to examine — and offer the least protection to minds the state has already decided to suspect.

This is not a critique that can be addressed by better drafting. McNaughton could be replaced with a volitional test, or a broader incapacity standard, or a purely medical determination. The conspiracy inference could be subjected to higher evidentiary standards, requiring corroboration beyond association. Common intention could be limited to cases where prior planning is specifically proved rather than inferred from presence and relationship.

All of these reforms are available in principle. None of them would resolve the fundamental problem, which is not in the provisions but in the system that applies them. The law cannot read minds. It infers mental states from evidence. The evidence it finds is determined by the evidence it looks for. And what it looks for is shaped by prior decisions about who deserves scrutiny — decisions made before the trial, before the charge, before the alleged offence, in the accumulated files and surveillance records and organisational profiles that the state maintains about the people it considers worth watching.

Macaulay wrote a Code that was, as RH10 traced, deliberately silent on moral foundations. He described acts and consequences and left the moral reasoning to the communities governed. What he could not build into the Code was a guarantee that the inferences drawn about mental states would be drawn with equal rigour across all accused, regardless of who they were and what the state already believed about them. That guarantee cannot live in a statute. It can only live in the institutional culture of the courts, prosecutors, and investigators who apply the statute — and the history traced across this essay series suggests that institutional culture has not always provided it.

The mind the law cannot read is everyone's mind equally. The mind the law chooses to scrutinise is not.
reflect: McNaughton asks whether the accused knew the nature of the act or knew it was wrong. It does not ask whether they could stop themselves. Design a replacement test for S.84 that addresses the volitional gap — one that a Pakistani court could actually apply with the evidence typically available in a criminal trial. What problems does your replacement test create that McNaughton avoids?

The essay argues that conspiracy prosecutions under S.120-A are profile-activated — practically triggered against people already in the state’s surveillance net, not against the invisible ordinary citizen. If that is true, does S.120-A serve its stated purpose of preventing dangerous organised conduct, or does it primarily serve as a tool for suppressing political and ideological opposition? Can it serve both simultaneously, and if so, does that make it legitimate or dangerous?

Section 34’s common intention and S.120-A’s conspiracy both attach liability to mental states inferred from conduct and associations rather than directly proved. The essay argues this gives the state discretion over whose mind to scrutinise. Is there a version of either provision that preserves the legitimate preventive and group-liability functions while meaningfully constraining that discretion? Or is the discretion structurally inseparable from the inference mechanism itself?
